content: A shell of mass 10 kg is moving with a velocity of 10 ms$$-$$1 when it blasts and forms two parts of mass 9 kg and 1 kg respectively. If the 1st mass is stationary, the velocity of the 2nd is
explanation:
v1 = 10 m/s;

m1 = 10 kg

v2 = 0;

m2 = 9 kg

v3 = v;

m3 = 1 kg

According to conservation of momentum

m1v1 = m2v2 + m3v3

10 $$\times$$ 10 = 9 $$\times$$ 0 + 1 $$\times$$ v;

v = 100 m/s

content: Force required to move a mass of 1 kg at rest on a horizontal rough plane ($$\mu$$ = 0.1 and g = 9.8 m/s2) is
explanation:
$${f_r} = \mu N = \mu mg = 0.1 \times 1 \times 9.8 = 0.98\,N$$

(Assuming that the value of $$\mu$$ = 0.1 is the coefficient of static friction.), so F > 0.98 N.

content: A rocket of mass 100 kg burns 0.1 kg of fuel per sec. If the velocity of exhaust gas is 1 km/sec, then it lifts with an acceleration of
explanation:
$${{dm} \over {dt}} = 0.1$$ kg/sec;

Mass of the rocket = 100 kg

$$v = 1$$ km/sec = 1000 m/sec.

$$F = {{d(mv)} \over {dt}}$$

$$ = m{{dv} \over {dt}} - v{{dm} \over {dt}} = 0$$ as the mass is decreasing

$$100a - 1000 \times 0.1 = 0$$

$$a = \, + 1$$ m/s2

content: A bullet emerges from a barrel of length 1.2 m with a speed of 640 ms$$-$$1. Assuming constant acceleration, the approximate time that it spends in the barrel after the gun is fired is
explanation:
To determine the approximate time the bullet spends in the barrel, we can use the kinematic equation which relates the final velocity, initial velocity, acceleration, and distance traveled:

$$v^2 = u^2 + 2as$$

where:

  • $$v$$ is the final velocity
  • $$u$$ is the initial velocity
  • $$a$$ is the acceleration
  • $$s$$ is the distance traveled

Given:

  • $$v = 640 \, \text{ms}^{-1}$$
  • $$u = 0 \, \text{ms}^{-1}$$ (since the bullet starts from rest)
  • $$s = 1.2 \, \text{m}$$

Substitute these values into the kinematic equation:

$$640^2 = 0^2 + 2 \cdot a \cdot 1.2$$

Simplifying, we get:

$$409600 = 2.4a$$

Solving for $$a$$:

$$a = \frac{409600}{2.4} \approx 170666.67 \, \text{ms}^{-2}$$

Now that we have the acceleration, we can determine the time $$t$$ using the equation:

$$v = u + at$$

Substitute the values:

$$640 = 0 + 170666.67 \cdot t$$

Simplifying, we get:

$$t = \frac{640}{170666.67} \approx 0.00375 \, \text{s}$$

Converting to milliseconds (ms):

$$0.00375 \, \text{s} = 3.75 \, \text{ms}$$

Rounding to the nearest option, the approximate time the bullet spends in the barrel is:

Option A: 4 ms

content: The minimum force required to start pushing a body up a rough (having co-efficient of friction $\mu$ ) inclined plane is $\vec{F}_1$ while the minimum force needed to prevent it from sliding is $\overrightarrow{F_2}$. If the inclined plane makes an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal such that $\tan \theta=2 \mu$, then the ratio $F_1 / F_2$ is
explanation:
Given:

  • Coefficient of friction = $\mu$

  • Angle of incline = $\theta$, where $\tan\theta = 2\mu$

Let the weight of the body be $mg$.

Step 1: Forces Acting on the Body

  • Along the plane:

  • Component of weight down the incline = $mg \sin\theta$

  • Normal reaction = $mg \cos\theta$

  • Frictional force = $\mu mg \cos\theta$

  • Let $F$ be the applied force parallel to the incline (as is usual in such problems).

A. Pushing the Body Up (Minimum $F_1$)

To just start moving up, applied force $F_1$ must overcome:

  • gravity component $mg\sin\theta$

  • plus friction (acts downward) $\mu mg\cos\theta$

So,

$ F_1 = mg\sin\theta + \mu mg\cos\theta $

B. Preventing from Sliding Down (Minimum $F_2$)

To just prevent sliding down, applied force $F_2$ must balance:

  • gravity component $mg\sin\theta$

  • minus friction (acts upward if object tends to slide down) $\mu mg\cos\theta$

So,

$ F_2 = mg\sin\theta - \mu mg\cos\theta $

C. Ratio $\dfrac{F_1}{F_2}$

$ \frac{F_1}{F_2} = \frac{mg\sin\theta + \mu mg\cos\theta}{mg\sin\theta - \mu mg\cos\theta} = \frac{\sin\theta + \mu \cos\theta}{\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta} $

D. Express in terms of $\tan\theta$

Divide numerator and denominator by $\cos\theta$:

$ = \frac{\tan\theta + \mu}{\tan\theta - \mu} $

Given: $\tan\theta = 2\mu$

So,

$ \frac{F_1}{F_2} = \frac{2\mu + \mu}{2\mu - \mu} = \frac{3\mu}{\mu} = 3 $

Final Answer:

$ \boxed{3} $

Option D is correct.

content: A force $\vec{F}=a \hat{i}+b \hat{j}+c \hat{k}$ is acting on a body of mass $m$. The body was initially at rest at the origin. The co-ordinates of the body after time ' $t$ ' will be
explanation:
Using Newton’s second law,

$$\vec F = m\vec a\;\Longrightarrow\;\vec a = \frac{\vec F}{m} = \Bigl(\frac a m,\;\frac b m,\;\frac c m\Bigr).$$

Starting from rest at the origin, the displacement after time $t$ is

$$\vec r(t)=\frac12\,\vec a\,t^2 = \frac12\Bigl(\frac a m,\;\frac b m,\;\frac c m\Bigr)t^2 =\Bigl(\frac{a\,t^2}{2m},\;\frac{b\,t^2}{2m},\;\frac{c\,t^2}{2m}\Bigr).$$

Hence the correct choice is Option A.

content: What force $$\mathrm{F}$$ is required to start moving this $$10 \mathrm{~kg}$$ block shown in the figure if it acts at an angle of $$60^{\circ}$$ as shown? $$(\mu_s=0.6)$$

A body of mass 2 kg moves in a horizontal circular path of radius 5 m. At an instant, its speed is 2$$\sqrt5$$ m/s and is increasing at the rate of 3 m/s$$^2$$. The magnitude of force acting on the body at that instant is,
explanation:
Given,

$$\begin{aligned} & m=2 \mathrm{~kg}, r=5 \mathrm{~m}, v=2 \sqrt{5} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\ & a_t=\frac{d v}{d t}=3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Centripetal acceleration or radial acceleration is

$$a_r=\frac{v^2}{r}=\frac{(2 \sqrt{5})^2}{5}=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$$

$$\therefore$$ Net acceleration, $$a=\sqrt{a_t^2+a_r^2}$$

$$\therefore \quad a=\sqrt{(3)^2+(4)^2}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$$

$$\therefore$$ The force action on the body is

$$F=m a=2 \times 5=10 \mathrm{~N}$$

content: If a string, suspended from the ceiling is given a downward force F1, its length becomes L1. Its length is L2, if the downward force is F2. What is its actual length?
explanation:
According to the Hooke's law, for deformation of string of length $$l$$,

$${{{F_1}} \over A} \propto {{\Delta {l_1}} \over l}$$ ....... (i)

and in second case,

$${{{F_2}} \over A} \propto {{\Delta {l_2}} \over l}$$ ...... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we have

$${{{F_1}} \over {{F_2}}} = {{\Delta {l_1}} \over {\Delta {l_2}}} = {{{l_1} - l} \over {{l_2} - l}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow ({F_2} - {F_1})l = {F_2}{l_1} - {F_1}{l_2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow l = {{{F_2}{l_1} - {F_1}{l_2}} \over {{F_2} - {F_1}}}$$

content: A golf ball of mass 50 gm placed on a tee, is struck by a golf-club. The speed of the golf ball as it leaves the tee is 100 m/s, the time of contact on the ball is 0.02 s. If the force decreases to zero linearly with time, then the force at the beginning of the contact is
explanation:
Given, initial velocity, $$u = 0$$ m/s

Final velocity, $$v = 100$$ m/s

Mass of ball, $$m = 50 \times {10^{ - 3}}$$ kg

Time of contact $$ = 0.02\,s$$

Impulse, $$F = {{m(v - u)} \over t}$$

$$ = {{50 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}(100 - 0)} \over {0.02}}$$

$$ = {{5 \times 100} \over 2}$$

$$ = 250\,N$$

content: Three blocks are pushed with a force F across a frictionless table as shown in figure above. Let N1 be the contact force between the left two blocks and N2 be the contact force between the right two blocks. Then,
explanation:
Given,


Since, the table is frictionless, hence acceleration of the whole system is given as

$$a = {F \over {m + 2m + 3m}} = {F \over {6m}}$$

Free body diagram at 1st block (left most)


From Newton's equation of motion,

$$F - {N_1} = ma = m \times {F \over {6m}}$$ $$\because$$ $$\left( {a = {F \over {6m}}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {N_1} = {{5F} \over 6}$$

Free body diagram of 3rd block (right most)


From Newton's equation of motion,

$${N_2} = 3ma = 3m \times {F \over {6m}}$$ $$\because$$ $$\left( {a = {F \over {6m}}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {N_2} = {F \over 2}$$

Hence, F > N1 > N2.

content: A block of mass m rests on a horizontal table with a coefficient of static friction $$\mu $$. What minimum force must be applied on the block to drag it on the table?
explanation:
The block diagram is as shown below,



According to above diagram,

mg = N + Fsin$$\theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ N = mg $$ - $$ Fsin$$\theta $$ ......(i)

and Fcos$$\theta $$ = $$\mu $$N

$$ \Rightarrow $$ Fcos$$\theta $$ = $$\mu $$(mg $$ - $$ Fsin$$\theta $$).............[using Eq. (i)]

For Fmin'

$${d \over {d\theta }}(\cos \theta + \mu \sin \theta ) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow - \sin \theta + \mu \cos \theta = 0$$

$$\tan \theta $$ = $$\mu $$

$$\sin \theta = {\mu \over {\sqrt {1 + {\mu ^2}} }}$$ and $$\cos \theta = {1 \over {\sqrt {1 + {\mu ^2}} }}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${F_{\min }} = {{\mu mg} \over {{1 \over {\sqrt {1 + {\mu ^2}} }} + {{\mu .\mu } \over {\sqrt {1 + {\mu ^2}} }}}}$$

$$ = {{\mu mg} \over {{{1 + {\mu ^2}} \over {\sqrt {1 + {\mu ^2}} }}}} = {{\mu mg} \over {\sqrt {1 + {\mu ^2}} }}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${F_{\min }} = {{\mu .mg} \over {\sqrt {1 + {\mu ^2}} }}$$

content: Two weights of the mass m<sub>1</sub> and m<sub>2</sub> (> m<sub>1</sub>) are joined by an inextensible string of negligible mass passing over a fixed frictionless pulley. The magnitude of the acceleration of the loads is
explanation:
According to the question, we can draw the following diagram


Hence, T is the tension in the string.

In equilibrium condition,

For mass m1, T $$-$$ m1g = m1a ... (i)

For mass m2, m2g $$-$$ T = m2a ....(ii)

After adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$\eqalign{ & {m_2}g - {m_1}g = {m_1}a + {m_2}a \cr \cr \Rightarrow& ({m_2} - {m_1})g = ({m_1} + {m_2})a \cr \cr \Rightarrow& a = {{({m_2} - {m_1})} \over {({m_1} + {m_2})}}g \cr} $$

content: A block of mass m<sub>2</sub> is placed on a horizontal table and another block of mass m<sub>1</sub> is placed on top of it. An increasing horizontal force F = at is exerted on the upper block but the lower block never moves as a result. If the coefficient of friction between the blocks is $$\mu$$<sub>1</sub> and that between the lower block and the table is $$\mu$$<sub>2</sub>, then what is the maximum possible value of $$\mu$$<sub>1</sub> / $$\mu$$<sub>2</sub> ?
explanation:
According to the question,


FBD of lower block of mass m2


$$ \therefore $$ $${N_2} = {m_2}g + {N_1} = ({m_1} + {m_2})g$$

$$ \because $$ Lower block never moves.

$$ \therefore $$ $${f_{{r_2}}} \ge {f_{{r_1}}}$$

$${\mu _2}{N_2} \ge {\mu _1}{N_1} \Rightarrow {\mu _2}({m_1} + {m_2})g \ge {\mu _1}{m_1}g$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{m_1} + {m_2}} \over {{m_1}}} \ge {{{\mu _1}} \over {{\mu _2}}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${{{\mu _1}} \over {{\mu _2}}} \le 1 + {{{m_2}} \over {{m_1}}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${\left. {{{{\mu _1}} \over {{\mu _2}}}} \right|_{\max }} = 1 + {{{m_2}} \over {{m_1}}}$$

content: The velocity (v) of a particle (under a force F) depends on its distance (x) from the origin (with x > 0) $$v \propto {1 \over {\sqrt x }}$$. Find how the magnitude of the force (F) on the particle depends on x?
explanation:
According to the question,

$$v \propto {1 \over {\sqrt x }}$$

or $$v \propto {k \over {\sqrt x }}$$ .... (i)

$${{dv} \over {dt}} = {d \over {dt}}\,.\,{K \over {\sqrt x }} = k\,.\,{{ - 1} \over 2}\,.\,{x^{ - {1 \over 2} - 1}}\,.\,{{dx} \over {dt}}$$

$$a = - {k \over 2}\,.\,{x^{ - {3 \over 2}}}\,.\,{k \over {\sqrt x }}$$ [$$ \because $$ $${{dx} \over {dt}} = v$$ and $$v = {k \over {\sqrt x }}$$ from Eq. (i)]

$$ = {{{k^2}} \over 2}\,.\,{1 \over {{x^2}}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ Force, $$F = Mass\, \times \,|Acceleration|$$

$$ = m{{{k^2}} \over 2}\,.\,{1 \over {{x^2}}}$$ (magnitude)

$$F = {{{k^2}} \over 2}\,.\,{m \over {{x^2}}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$F \propto {1 \over {{x^2}}}$$

content: A block of mass 1 kg starts from rest at x = 0 and moves along the X-axis under the action of a force F = kt, where t is time and k = 1 Ns<sup>$$-$$1</sup>. The distance the block will travel in 6 seconds is
explanation:
From Newton's second law

F = ma

m = 1 kg

F = 1 $$ \times $$ a = $${{dv} \over {dt}}$$

According to the question,

F = kt = $${{dv} \over {dt}}$$

dv = kt . dt

Integration gives,

$$v = {{k{t^2}} \over 2} = {{dx} \over {dt}}$$

$$x = {{k{t^3}} \over 6} = {{1 \times 6 \times 6 \times 6} \over 6} = 36$$ m

content: A mass of 1 kg is suspended by means of a thread. The system is (i) lifted up with an acceleration of 4.9 ms<sup>$$-$$2</sup>. (ii) lowered with an acceleration of 4.9 ms<sup>$$-$$2</sup>. The ratio of tension in the first and second case is
explanation:
(i) When the body of mass m lifted up the forces acting on the body



$${T_1} - mg = {{mg} \over 2}$$

$${T_1} = mg + {{mg} \over 2} = {{3mg} \over 2}$$

(ii) When the body lowered the acting forces



$$(mg - {T_2}) = {{mg} \over 2}$$

$${T_2} = mg - {{mg} \over 2} = {{mg} \over 2}$$

On dividing Eq. (i) from Eq (ii) we get

$$ \therefore $$ $${{{T_1}} \over {{T_2}}} = {{3mg/2} \over {mg/2}} = {3 \over 1}$$

= 3 : 1

content: Two spheres $S_1$ and $S_2$ of masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ respectively collide with each other. Initially $S_1$ is at rest and $S_2$ is moving with velocity $v$ along $x$-axis. After collision $S_2$ has a velocity $\frac{v}{2}$ in a direction perpendicular to the original direction. The sphere $S_1$ moves after collision

options.content: with a velocity of magnitude $\frac{m_2}{m_1} v \frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}$.
with a velocity in the direction $\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{3}\right)$ to the $x$-axis.
with a velocity whose direction makes an angle $\theta$ with the $x$-axis such that $\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)$ or $\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)$.
with a velocity of magnitude $\frac{m_1}{2 m_2} v \sqrt{5}$.

explanation:
Let $\vec v_1=(v_{1x},v_{1y})$ be the post‐collision velocity of $S_1$. By conservation of momentum (taking $+\hat\imath$ along the original $x$–axis and $+\hat\jmath$ along the new direction of $S_2$),

Along $x$:

$m_1\,v_{1x}+m_2\cdot0=m_2\,v$

$\displaystyle\implies v_{1x}=\frac{m_2}{m_1}\,v$.

Along $y$:

$m_1\,v_{1y}+m_2\,(v/2)=0$

$\displaystyle\implies v_{1y}=-\frac{m_2}{2m_1}\,v$.

Hence

$$ \vec v_1 =\frac{m_2\,v}{m_1}\,\hat\imath \;-\;\frac{m_2\,v}{2m_1}\,\hat\jmath. $$

Its magnitude is

$$ |\vec v_1| =\sqrt{\bigl(\tfrac{m_2}{m_1}v\bigr)^2+\bigl(\tfrac{m_2}{2m_1}v\bigr)^2} =\frac{m_2}{m_1}\,v\;\frac{\sqrt5}{2}, $$

and its direction $\theta$ (measured from the $+x$–axis toward $+y$) satisfies

$$ \tan\theta =\frac{v_{1y}}{v_{1x}} =-\frac12 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \theta=\arctan\!\Bigl(-\tfrac12\Bigr). $$

• The magnitude matches Option A.

• The direction matches the “$\tan^{-1}(-\tfrac12)$” part of Option C.
